title: Datascope CS100 / CS100i Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Battery Failure or Poor Runtime
description: Troubleshooting CS100 / CS100i battery alarms, poor battery runtime, charging concerns, or unexpected loss of battery operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the IABP is not being evaluated on an active patient unless clinical staff have already secured alternate support. If the pump is in use and battery reliability is questionable, keep the unit connected to verified AC power and have clinical staff prepare another functional IABP if transport or battery operation may be needed. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained without relying on an unstable battery. If this resolves the immediate risk, stop troubleshooting during patient use and arrange bench evaluation when safe.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Battery Issue
steps.instructions: Verify what was reported: Battery failure alarm; Battery not charging; Short runtime; Unit shuts down on battery; Battery indicator drops quickly; Pump only operates on AC power; Battery fault occurs during transport or unplugging. Expected outcome: The complaint is clearly identified before testing. Why it matters: “Battery failure” may be a true battery issue, an AC charging issue, or a power connection problem.

steps.title: Check AC Power Connection First
steps.instructions: Confirm the CS100 / CS100i power cord is fully seated at the pump and plugged into a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Look for evidence that the unit is receiving AC power, such as AC indicator status, charging indication, or normal operation while plugged in. Expected outcome: The pump recognizes AC power and charging should be possible. If the AC connection was loose or the outlet was bad and the unit begins charging normally, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Test the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug the unit into a different verified outlet, preferably on emergency power if clinically appropriate. Avoid assuming battery failure until the power source has been ruled out. Expected outcome: The pump should recognize AC power consistently on a known-good outlet. If the issue only occurs at one outlet, report the outlet concern to Facilities and document the device as functional on another outlet.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for: Bent blades; Loose plug fit; Cuts or crushed insulation; Damaged strain relief; Signs of overheating; Intermittent AC detection when the cord is moved. If available, test with a known-good compatible power cord. Expected outcome: AC power should remain stable with no flickering, resets, or intermittent charging indication. If replacing the power cord resolves the issue, remove the damaged cord from service and stop.

steps.title: Allow Adequate Charge Time
steps.instructions: If the unit was recently unplugged, stored, transported, or left off charge, connect it to AC power and allow sufficient charging time per site practice. Do not immediately fail the battery solely because the indicator is low after storage. Expected outcome: Battery charge level should increase and battery fault behavior should clear after charging. If the battery accepts charge and runtime returns to normal, document low charge due to storage or unplugged condition and stop.

steps.title: Check for Charging Indication
steps.instructions: While connected to AC power, confirm whether the pump shows charging activity or battery status improvement. Watch for: No charge indication; Battery fault alarm while on AC; Charge indicator stuck low; Unit operating normally on AC but not charging; Battery icon or status behaving abnormally. Expected outcome: The device should show that the battery is being charged or maintained while connected to AC. If there is no charging indication with verified AC input, escalation is appropriate.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Battery Runtime Check
steps.instructions: Only perform a battery runtime check when the unit is not supporting a patient. With the unit fully charged or charged as much as it will accept, disconnect AC power and observe whether the unit remains powered on battery. Check for: Immediate shutdown; Battery alarm; Rapid battery drop; Runtime significantly below expected clinical use; Pump reset or unstable operation. Expected outcome: The pump should continue operating on battery long enough to support safe transport and temporary AC interruption. If the unit shuts down quickly or runtime is poor, remove it from service.

steps.title: Check for Excessive Heat, Odor, or Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect the battery area and power inlet externally for unusual heat, odor, swelling, residue, or signs of electrical damage. Do not open the device for internal inspection during initial troubleshooting. Expected outcome: No abnormal heat, smell, or visible damage should be present. If heat, smell, smoke, or physical battery damage is suspected, disconnect from use when safe, label Out of Service, and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Review Battery Age and Maintenance History
steps.instructions: Check the asset history for: Battery replacement date; Prior runtime failures; Recent power-related work orders; Storage off charge; Repeated transport failures; Missed battery checks or PM findings. Expected outcome: History may confirm whether the battery is aged, repeatedly failing, or recently replaced. If repeated battery complaints are documented, do not return the unit to service without repair evaluation.

steps.title: Verify Final Device Status
steps.instructions: If the battery charges normally and passes a safe runtime check, return the device to service only if it meets your department’s acceptance criteria. If there is any doubt about battery reliability, especially for transport use, remove the device from service. Expected outcome: The pump is either verified operational or appropriately escalated.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Datascope CS100 / CS100i IABP displayed a battery failure alarm and had poor runtime during transport preparation.
CCR.Cause: Verified AC outlet and power cord were functional, but the pump battery did not hold charge and the unit showed rapid battery depletion during bench testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the IABP from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for battery/charging system evaluation and repair.

helpfulDetails: Whether emergency power or another outlet was used
Whether the power cord was inspected or swapped
Battery alarm text or indicator behavior
Whether the unit shut down immediately on battery
Any unusual heat, odor, sound, or visible damage
Whether the device was returned to service or removed from service

title: Datascope CS100 / CS100i Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - ECG Trigger Detection Failure
description: Troubleshooting ECG trigger failure caused by leads, cables, patient signal quality, settings, or acquisition issues before repair escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is hemodynamically supported and that the Datascope CS100 / CS100i is not losing reliable timing during active therapy. If ECG triggering is unstable or unavailable during patient support, notify clinical staff immediately and have them select an appropriate alternate trigger source, such as arterial pressure trigger if clinically suitable. Expected outcome: Patient support continues safely while the ECG trigger issue is evaluated.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Problem
steps.instructions: Confirm the issue is specifically ECG trigger detection failure, not poor augmentation, helium leak, console alarm silence, or a general timing issue. Observe the ECG waveform and trigger indicators on the pump display. Expected outcome: The pump either fails to detect ECG consistently, does not trigger from the ECG waveform, or intermittently loses ECG trigger.

steps.title: Do Not Troubleshoot on an Unsafe Active Patient Setup
steps.instructions: If the pump is actively supporting a patient and ECG trigger failure is causing unstable assist timing, do not perform extended troubleshooting at the bedside. Have clinical staff stabilize therapy using another appropriate trigger mode, another IABP, or alternate support plan as required. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting only continues when patient safety is protected.

steps.title: Check ECG Lead Placement and Skin Contact
steps.instructions: Inspect the ECG electrodes on the patient for loose pads, dried gel, poor adhesion, excessive hair, sweat, or poor skin prep. Ask clinical staff to replace electrodes and confirm proper lead placement. Expected outcome: A clean ECG waveform appears and the pump detects trigger events reliably. If this resolves the issue, document the electrode or skin contact problem and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Lead Wires
steps.instructions: Check the ECG lead wires connected to the pump for bent pins, loose snaps, damaged insulation, pulled strain reliefs, or intermittent signal when the cable is gently moved. Expected outcome: Lead wires remain secure and ECG signal does not drop out during movement. If replacing the lead wires restores ECG trigger detection, remove the failed lead set from service and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct ECG Cable Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Verify that the ECG patient cable or trunk cable is compatible with the CS100 / CS100i and is fully seated in the correct ECG input. Look for partially inserted connectors, damaged connector housings, or cables connected to the wrong input. Expected outcome: The pump receives ECG through the proper input path.

steps.title: Swap to a Known-Good ECG Cable or Lead Set
steps.instructions: Replace the ECG cable and lead set with a known-good set from a working configuration. Expected outcome: ECG trigger detection returns if the original cable or lead set was defective. If the issue follows the cable, tag the cable as failed and remove it from service.

steps.title: Check ECG Waveform Quality
steps.instructions: Observe the ECG waveform for low amplitude, artifact, baseline wander, pacing spikes, excessive noise, or poor QRS definition. A trigger failure may occur if the pump cannot reliably identify the QRS complex. Expected outcome: The ECG waveform is stable enough for trigger detection.

steps.title: Check for Electrical or Motion Artifact
steps.instructions: Look for artifact caused by patient movement, bed movement, loose electrodes, nearby equipment, poor cable routing, or cables stretched across powered equipment. Reposition the ECG cable away from power cords, pumps, warming devices, and moving bed sections when possible. Expected outcome: ECG artifact decreases and trigger detection improves.

steps.title: Verify ECG Lead Selection
steps.instructions: Check whether the selected ECG lead provides a clear QRS complex. If the current lead has low amplitude or excessive artifact, have clinical staff select another appropriate lead with a stronger QRS signal. Expected outcome: The selected ECG lead produces reliable trigger detection. If changing ECG lead selection resolves the issue, stop and document the lead used.

steps.title: Check Trigger Mode and Timing Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is actually set to ECG trigger mode and not another trigger source. Review the display for trigger source, assist ratio, timing markers, and any related messages. Expected outcome: The pump is configured to trigger from ECG and the timing markers correspond to the detected QRS complexes.

steps.title: Assess Paced Rhythm Considerations
steps.instructions: If the patient has a pacemaker or pacing spikes are present, confirm whether the ECG waveform allows reliable QRS detection. Pacing artifacts, low intrinsic QRS amplitude, or wide complex rhythms may interfere with ECG triggering. Expected outcome: Trigger detection is assessed based on the actual rhythm and waveform quality. If ECG trigger is not reliable due to patient rhythm or pacing, clinical staff should use an appropriate alternate trigger source.

steps.title: Compare With Another ECG Source
steps.instructions: Compare the pump ECG waveform with the bedside monitor ECG. If the bedside monitor shows a clean ECG but the CS100 / CS100i does not, suspect the pump cable, ECG input, or pump acquisition path. If both devices show poor ECG quality, suspect electrodes, patient lead placement, artifact, or patient rhythm. Expected outcome: The fault is narrowed to either the patient setup or the IABP ECG acquisition path.

steps.title: Check for Intermittent Connector Failure
steps.instructions: Gently manipulate the ECG connector at the pump input and along the cable strain relief while observing the waveform. Do not force or stress the connector. Expected outcome: The ECG signal remains stable. If ECG drops out when the connector is moved, remove the device or cable from service depending on where the fault is isolated.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the pump is not supporting an active patient, or if clinical staff confirm it is safe, restart the unit and allow it to initialize normally. Expected outcome: A temporary software or initialization issue clears and ECG acquisition returns. If ECG trigger detection still fails after restart with known-good cables and good electrodes, escalate.

steps.title: Test With Known-Good Accessories During Bench Evaluation
steps.instructions: If removed from patient use, test the CS100 / CS100i with known-good ECG cables and an ECG simulator or approved test setup. Expected outcome: The pump detects ECG trigger events from a known-good signal. If the unit fails to detect ECG from a simulator or known-good source, the issue is likely internal and should not be troubleshot further at the bedside.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Datascope CS100 / CS100i would not reliably trigger from the ECG signal during IABP therapy.
CCR.Cause: ECG trigger failure was traced to a damaged ECG lead cable that caused intermittent waveform dropout when moved.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the ECG cable with a known-good cable, confirmed stable ECG waveform and trigger detection, and removed the defective cable from service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the pump was in active patient use
Presence of artifact, pacing spikes, or low QRS amplitude
ECG simulator tested, if bench evaluated
Whether the issue followed the cable or stayed with the pump
Whether the unit was returned to service or sent for repair

title: Datascope CS100 / CS100i Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Arterial Pressure Trigger Failure
description: Troubleshooting arterial pressure trigger failure caused by setup, cable, transducer, pressure source, or signal quality issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is hemodynamically supported and not relying on an unstable trigger source. If the Datascope CS100 / CS100i is actively supporting a patient and arterial pressure triggering is unreliable, notify clinical staff immediately and have them select an appropriate alternate trigger source, such as ECG trigger, if clinically acceptable. Expected: Patient support continues without relying on an unstable arterial pressure trigger.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Trigger Problem
steps.instructions: Verify the issue being reported: Arterial pressure trigger will not select; Pump does not recognize arterial pressure waveform; Trigger marker is absent or inconsistent; IAB inflation/deflation timing is erratic; Poor augmentation occurs only when using AP trigger. Expected: The failure is confirmed as arterial pressure trigger related, not a general pump, timing, or helium delivery issue.

steps.title: Verify the Arterial Pressure Waveform Quality
steps.instructions: Observe the arterial pressure waveform on the IABP display. Look for: Adequate pulse pressure; Clear systolic upstroke; Stable baseline; No excessive whip, damping, artifact, or noise; Reasonable pressure scale and displayed numeric value. Expected: A clean arterial waveform should be visible and usable for triggering. If the waveform is absent, flat, severely damped, or noisy, correct the arterial pressure setup before continuing.

steps.title: Check That the Patient Has a Usable Arterial Pressure Source
steps.instructions: Confirm with clinical staff that the arterial line is functioning properly. Check for obvious external issues: Stopcock closed or incorrectly positioned; Pressure tubing kinked or clamped; Loose pressure cable connection; Air bubbles in the pressure line; Damped waveform from clotting, catheter position, or poor flush setup; Transducer not leveled or zeroed. Expected: The bedside arterial pressure system produces a reliable waveform before the IABP uses it as a trigger. If clinical staff correct the arterial line setup and the trigger becomes stable, document the issue and stop.

steps.title: Verify IABP Pressure Cable Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the arterial pressure input cable connected to the Datascope CS100 / CS100i. Confirm: Cable is fully seated at the IABP; Cable is fully seated at the transducer or pressure output source; Connector pins are not bent, recessed, wet, or contaminated; Cable strain relief is intact; Cable does not cut in and out when gently moved. Expected: The arterial pressure signal remains stable when the cable and connectors are gently manipulated. If reseating the cable restores triggering, document the loose connection and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Pressure Source and Cable Type
steps.instructions: Verify the IABP is connected to the correct arterial pressure output, not an incorrect invasive pressure channel or non-pressure port. Confirm the cable being used is appropriate for the Datascope CS100 / CS100i and the connected monitoring or transducer system. Expected: The IABP receives a valid arterial pressure signal from a compatible source. If the wrong cable or output was used, replace with the correct setup, retest AP trigger, document, and stop.

steps.title: Swap the Arterial Pressure Cable
steps.instructions: Replace the arterial pressure input cable with a known-good compatible cable. Expected: If the original cable was faulty, the waveform and arterial pressure trigger should become stable with the known-good cable. If the issue follows the cable, remove the faulty cable from service and stop.

steps.title: Compare Against the Bedside Monitor Pressure Waveform
steps.instructions: Compare the arterial waveform displayed on the bedside monitor to the waveform displayed on the IABP. Look for differences: Bedside monitor has a good waveform, but IABP does not; IABP waveform is noisy while bedside monitor is stable; IABP shows no pressure value from the same source; Both devices show poor waveform quality. Expected: The IABP waveform should reasonably match the bedside arterial waveform. If both devices show poor waveform quality, the issue is likely with the arterial line, transducer, tubing, or patient signal source.

steps.title: Check Trigger Mode and Timing Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm the CS100 / CS100i is actually set to arterial pressure trigger mode and that the trigger source was not changed inadvertently. Review visible settings with clinical staff as needed, including assist ratio and timing behavior. Expected: The selected trigger mode matches the intended arterial pressure trigger setup. If the wrong trigger mode was selected, correct the selection, verify stable triggering, document, and stop.

steps.title: Evaluate for Low Pulse Pressure or Physiologic Limitation
steps.instructions: Confirm the arterial waveform has enough pulse pressure for reliable trigger detection. Poor AP triggering may occur when the waveform is technically present but clinically difficult to detect, such as with: Very low pulse pressure; Severe hypotension; Dampened arterial line; Arrhythmias; Excessive patient movement; Poor catheter position. Expected: AP trigger works best with a clear, repeatable arterial waveform. If the pressure signal is not suitable, clinical staff should use an appropriate alternate trigger source.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Pressure Signal Source When Safe
steps.instructions: If available and appropriate for bench evaluation, connect the IABP to a known-good pressure simulator or verified test source. Expected: The CS100 / CS100i should display a stable pressure waveform and recognize AP trigger from a proper test signal. If the pump works with the test source, the issue is likely external to the IABP.

steps.title: Inspect for Signs of Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the AP input area and nearby connectors for: Impact damage; Fluid intrusion; Corrosion; Broken connector shell; Loose input jack; Unusual heat or smell. Expected: No visible damage or contamination should be present. If damage or fluid intrusion is found, remove the device from service and send for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Functional Check After Correction
steps.instructions: After any external correction, verify: Arterial waveform is visible; Trigger marker is consistent; Pump timing is stable; No AP trigger-related alarms return; Augmentation appears appropriate for the clinical setup; Device passes applicable operational checks before return to service. Expected: The device operates normally with a stable arterial pressure trigger. If the issue is resolved, document the corrected cause and final device status.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Datascope CS100 / CS100i would not trigger reliably from the arterial pressure waveform.
CCR.Cause: Found the arterial pressure input cable intermittently lost signal when moved at the transducer connection.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the arterial pressure cable with a known-good compatible cable, verified stable AP waveform and trigger detection, and returned the IABP to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether patient was transferred to alternate support or alternate trigger source
Exact AP trigger alarm or message displayed
Arterial waveform quality on bedside monitor
Any intermittent behavior during cable movement
Signs of fluid intrusion, heat, smell, or physical damage

title: Datascope CS100 / CS100i Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Helium Leak or Poor Augmentation
description: Troubleshooting helium leak alarms, poor balloon augmentation, low assist pressure, or reduced IABP performance from external causes.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is clinically stable and not dependent on an unresolved IABP fault during troubleshooting. If helium leak alarms, poor augmentation, or inconsistent assist occurs during active therapy, notify clinical staff immediately and have them assess the patient and therapy need. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained safely before equipment checks begin. If the patient cannot safely remain on the suspect pump, transfer therapy to another verified IABP system per clinical protocol and remove the affected unit from use.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Review the reported symptom on the Datascope CS100 / CS100i: Helium leak alarm; Loss of helium pressure; Poor augmentation; Low diastolic augmentation; Weak assist waveform; Repeated fill or gas-related alarms; Balloon not inflating consistently. Expected outcome: The exact complaint is verified before parts are swapped or the device is removed.

steps.title: Check the Helium Cylinder Supply
steps.instructions: Verify that the helium cylinder is installed, open, seated correctly, and has adequate pressure. Confirm the cylinder is the correct type for the pump and is not empty or near empty. Expected outcome: The pump has a valid helium source available. If the cylinder is empty or low, replace it with a full approved cylinder, retest operation, and stop if the issue is resolved.

steps.title: Inspect the Helium Cylinder Connection
steps.instructions: Check the cylinder connection for looseness, cross-threading, damaged sealing surfaces, or signs that the cylinder is not fully seated. Listen for audible gas leakage at the cylinder connection area. Expected outcome: No hissing, loose fitting, or visible connection problem is found. If tightening or reseating the cylinder resolves the alarm or poor augmentation, document the correction and perform normal operational checks before returning the pump to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Balloon Catheter Tubing Path
steps.instructions: Trace the IAB catheter tubing from the patient connection to the pump connection without disturbing patient care. Look for kinks, crushed tubing, loose connections, partially disconnected fittings, moisture, blood, or damaged tubing. Expected outcome: The tubing path is clear, intact, and fully connected. If a kinked or loose external connection is found and corrected by qualified clinical staff, verify that augmentation returns to expected performance.

steps.title: Check the Catheter Connection at the Pump
steps.instructions: Confirm the catheter tubing is connected securely to the pump’s pneumatic connection. Check for incomplete engagement, damaged connector threads, loose fittings, or contamination at the connection point. Expected outcome: The catheter connection is secure and free of obvious damage. If the connection was loose and reseating resolves the leak alarm or weak augmentation, stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Verify Balloon Size and Setup Information
steps.instructions: Confirm that the balloon size and setup selections match the patient’s IAB catheter and clinical configuration. Incorrect setup information may affect inflation behavior and augmentation quality. Expected outcome: Pump setup matches the catheter and therapy configuration. If setup was incorrect, have qualified staff correct the setting, reassess augmentation, and document the correction.

steps.title: Review Trigger Source and Timing
steps.instructions: Poor augmentation may be caused by timing or trigger instability rather than a helium leak. Verify that ECG or arterial pressure triggering is stable and that inflation and deflation timing are clinically appropriate. Expected outcome: Triggering is stable and IAB inflation occurs at the proper point in the cardiac cycle. If augmentation improves after clinical timing or trigger correction, the issue was not primarily a helium supply fault.

steps.title: Check for Patient or Physiologic Causes
steps.instructions: Confirm with clinical staff whether poor augmentation may be related to patient condition rather than pump failure. Low blood pressure, arrhythmias, tachycardia, poor arterial waveform quality, catheter position concerns, or low vascular compliance may reduce augmentation. Expected outcome: Equipment-related causes are separated from patient-related causes. If the pump operates normally but augmentation remains poor, clinical evaluation of catheter position and patient condition is required.

steps.title: Inspect for External Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the pump exterior, pneumatic connection area, helium compartment, and catheter connection points for signs of impact, fluid intrusion, residue, or damaged fittings. Expected outcome: No external damage or contamination is present. If fluid intrusion, damaged connectors, or impact damage is found, remove the pump from service.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If the pump is not connected to an active patient and clinical staff approve, restart the device and observe whether the helium leak or gas-related alarm returns during startup or test operation. Expected outcome: The device completes startup without recurring helium or pneumatic faults. If the alarm returns with no patient connected and a verified helium supply installed, suspect an internal pneumatic or gas system fault.

steps.title: Test with Known Good External Components When Available
steps.instructions: On the bench or when not connected to a patient, test the pump using known good external accessories, cylinder, and approved test setup. Do not perform internal pneumatic disassembly during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: The pump either operates normally with known good external components or repeats the fault. If the fault follows the external component, replace that component. If the fault stays with the pump, escalate for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported a Datascope CS100i IABP was alarming for helium leak and providing weak augmentation during use.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found the helium cylinder was near empty and the cylinder connection required reseating before stable pressure was restored.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced the helium cylinder, reseated the connection, verified stable operation, and returned the pump to service after functional checks.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested if power concerns were also reported
Catheter tubing checked for kinks or loose fittings
Assist ratio and trigger source at time of complaint
ECG or arterial pressure trigger stability
Any audible hissing or gas smell
External damage, fluid intrusion, or contamination
Whether issue occurred on patient or during bench test

title: Datascope CS100 / CS100i Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Display, Keypad, or Printer Failure
description: Troubleshooting display, keypad, control input, or printer issues affecting visibility, operation, or documentation on a Datascope CS100 / CS100i IABP.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Datascope CS100 / CS100i is actively supporting a patient. If the pump is in use and the display, keypad, or control interface is unreliable, do not continue troubleshooting on the active patient unless clinical staff confirm it is safe. If therapy visibility or control is compromised, have clinical staff transfer the patient to another verified IABP before removing or troubleshooting the suspect unit. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained on a safe, functional device. If this resolves the immediate clinical risk, stop and document the transfer.

steps.title: Clarify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask clinical staff what failed and when it occurred: Blank or dim display; Frozen screen; Touch/control response issue, if applicable; Keypad buttons not responding; Intermittent button response; Printer not printing; Printer paper not feeding; Poor print quality; Issue occurs at startup, during transport, or during active therapy. Expected outcome: The reported issue is narrowed to display, keypad/control panel, printer, or a general system fault.

steps.title: Check Power Status and Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is connected to a known-good AC outlet and that the power cord is fully seated. Observe whether the unit powers on normally, completes startup, and shows normal indicators or alarms. Expected outcome: The pump powers up consistently with normal display and control response. If the display/keypad issue clears after restoring proper power, stop and document the power-related cause.

steps.title: Inspect the Display Externally
steps.instructions: Check the screen for: Cracks; Liquid intrusion; Physical impact damage; Severe dimness; Flickering; Missing sections or lines; Display visible only at certain angles; Evidence of cleaning fluid damage. Adjust normal brightness or contrast settings only if available through standard user-accessible controls. Expected outcome: The display is physically intact and readable. If the screen is damaged, remove the device from service and send for repair.

steps.title: Check for a Frozen or Locked Interface
steps.instructions: If the screen is visible but controls do not respond, determine whether the pump is actually frozen or whether only one keypad area is affected. Look for changing values, alarms, status indicators, or other signs that the system is still operating. Expected outcome: The issue is identified as either a full user-interface lockup or a localized keypad/control problem. If the unit is frozen during active patient support, transfer therapy to another pump before further evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect the Keypad and Control Panel
steps.instructions: Check the keypad or control area for: Stuck buttons; Torn overlay; Fluid residue; Cleaning chemical damage; Cracked plastic; Buttons that feel collapsed or unusually stiff; Buttons that respond intermittently when pressed. Do not force buttons or attempt to remove overlays at the bedside. Expected outcome: External keypad damage or contamination is identified or ruled out. If damage or fluid intrusion is found, remove the pump from service.

steps.title: Test Basic Control Response Off Patient
steps.instructions: Once the device is not supporting a patient, power the unit on and check whether normal menu navigation and control inputs respond. Test only standard user-accessible functions needed to confirm response. Do not initiate therapy testing unless it is appropriate for your facility procedure and test setup. Expected outcome: Keypad and menu response are consistent. If controls remain unresponsive or intermittent, escalate for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Active Alarms or System Messages
steps.instructions: Review the screen for any alarm, service message, printer message, battery message, or system fault that may explain the failure. Document the exact wording of any message. Expected outcome: Any visible alarm or fault message is captured accurately. If a service or system fault is present, remove the unit from service and send for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect Printer Paper and Door
steps.instructions: If the issue is printer-related, check that the printer door is fully closed and that approved paper is loaded correctly. Confirm the paper is not jammed, wrinkled, backward, or depleted. Expected outcome: Paper is loaded properly and the printer door is latched. If reloading paper restores printing, stop and document the paper-loading issue.

steps.title: Check for Paper Feed Obstruction
steps.instructions: Open the printer area only as intended for normal paper replacement. Look for small paper fragments, torn paper, adhesive residue, or misrouted paper. Do not disassemble the printer mechanism at the bedside. Expected outcome: The paper path is clear. If the obstruction is removed and the printer feeds normally, stop and document the obstruction.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Printer Function Check
steps.instructions: With the device off patient and safe to test, attempt a standard printout or strip recording using the normal user-accessible print function. Observe whether the printer: Starts normally; Feeds paper; Prints blank; Prints faintly; Prints intermittently; Makes abnormal noise; Stops immediately. Expected outcome: Printer behavior is confirmed and documented. If the printer still fails with correct paper and no obstruction, escalate for repair.

steps.title: Check for Environmental or Handling Factors
steps.instructions: Ask whether the failure occurred after: Transport; Cleaning; Liquid spill; Drop or impact; Storage on battery; Power interruption; Paper replacement; Use in a high-humidity or contaminated area. Expected outcome: A likely external trigger is identified. If impact, spill, or cleaning-fluid exposure is suspected, remove the device from service.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Clinically Safe
steps.instructions: If the pump is not connected to a patient, perform a normal power cycle according to facility practice. Confirm whether the display initializes, keypad responds, and printer functions after restart. Expected outcome: Temporary software or interface lockup is cleared. If the failure returns or remains intermittent, do not return the pump to service.

steps.title: Verify Final Device Status
steps.instructions: If the display, keypad, and printer pass functional checks and no abnormal messages remain, return the unit according to facility policy. If any display visibility issue, keypad response issue, printer failure, or intermittent control problem remains, remove the device from service. Expected outcome: The device is either confirmed functional or escalated appropriately.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Datascope CS100 / CS100i display was intermittently freezing and the keypad did not always respond during setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no power issue or paper problem, but keypad response remained intermittent during off-patient functional testing.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the IABP from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench repair evaluation of the user interface/control panel assembly.

helpfulDetails: Whether the pump was on a patient
Display condition: blank, dim, frozen, flickering, cracked, or readable
Keypad behavior: no response, intermittent response, stuck button, or damaged overlay
Printer behavior: no print, no feed, faint print, paper jam, abnormal noise
Evidence of liquid, cleaning residue, impact, heat, or odor
Whether a power cycle changed the behavior

title: Datascope System 98 / 98XT Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Pump Will Not Complete Self-Test
description: Troubleshooting failed self-test caused by power, gas supply, cable, accessory, setup, or device fault conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Datascope System 98 / 98XT is not connected to or supporting an active patient. If the pump is in clinical use or urgently needed, have staff obtain another verified intra-aortic balloon pump before troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained using a safe alternate device. If resolved by using another pump for patient care, stop troubleshooting the in-use situation and document the issue.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Self-Test Failure
steps.instructions: Power the pump on and observe the self-test sequence from a normal startup condition. Note whether the self-test stops immediately, fails partway through, repeats, displays an error, alarms, or shuts the unit down. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed and the exact behavior is documented. Why it matters: “Will not complete self-test” can be caused by several different external or internal conditions.

steps.title: Verify AC Power Source
steps.instructions: Confirm the pump is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated at the wall and at the pump. Test the outlet with another device or outlet tester if appropriate. Expected outcome: The pump receives stable AC power during startup. If the pump completes self-test on a different verified outlet, remove the faulty outlet from use and notify Facilities. Stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Power Cord and Plug
steps.instructions: Check the AC cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose strain relief, burn marks, or signs of overheating. Swap with an approved compatible power cord if available. Expected outcome: Damaged or intermittent AC cord issues are ruled out. If replacing the cord allows the pump to complete self-test, replace the defective cord and perform required safety/function checks. Stop.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition and Charging Status
steps.instructions: Observe battery indicators, low-battery messages, or abnormal startup behavior on battery power. If the unit has been stored unplugged, connect it to AC power and allow charging per facility practice before repeating self-test. Expected outcome: A depleted or weak battery is not preventing startup or self-test completion. If the pump completes self-test after adequate charging, document low battery/storage condition and return only after required checks. Stop.

steps.title: Inspect Helium Supply
steps.instructions: Confirm the helium cylinder is installed correctly, has adequate pressure, and is not empty. Verify the cylinder valve is open and that the regulator/cylinder connection is secure. Expected outcome: The pump has an adequate helium source for pneumatic checks during self-test. If replacing or opening the helium cylinder resolves the self-test failure, document the helium supply issue. Stop.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Pneumatic Connection Problems
steps.instructions: Inspect external helium tubing, fittings, and accessible connections for looseness, kinks, damage, or improper seating. Do not open the pump or perform internal pneumatic repair during initial troubleshooting. Expected outcome: External pneumatic restrictions or leaks are ruled out. If reseating an external connection allows self-test to complete, perform a functional check and document the correction. Stop.

steps.title: Remove Unneeded External Accessories
steps.instructions: Disconnect nonessential external connections such as recorder cables, data cables, auxiliary cables, or external trigger cables. Leave only required power and normal startup connections. Restart the pump and repeat self-test. Expected outcome: A faulty accessory or external connection is not interfering with startup. If self-test completes after removing an accessory, reconnect one item at a time to identify the suspect accessory. Stop once identified.

steps.title: Inspect ECG/AP Trigger Cables and Patient Input Connections
steps.instructions: Check ECG lead cables, pressure input cables, and external trigger cables for damage, bent pins, contamination, or loose connectors. Remove questionable cables and repeat self-test if the pump allows self-test without them. Expected outcome: A damaged signal cable or connector is not causing the pump to fail its startup checks. If removing or replacing a cable resolves the issue, tag the defective cable and document the replacement. Stop.

steps.title: Check Control Panel and Display Response
steps.instructions: During startup, verify that the display illuminates, buttons respond normally, and no keys appear stuck. Look for liquid intrusion, cracked overlays, damaged knobs, stuck buttons, or abnormal display behavior. Expected outcome: The control interface is usable and not preventing self-test completion. If a stuck key or damaged interface is found, remove the pump from service for repair evaluation. Stop.

steps.title: Listen and Observe During Self-Test
steps.instructions: Note unusual sounds such as repeated clicking, compressor/pneumatic cycling, no pneumatic activity, alarms, fan noise changes, or sudden shutdown. Check for unusual heat, burning odor, smoke, or visible damage. Expected outcome: Abnormal mechanical, pneumatic, or electrical symptoms are identified without disassembly. If heat, odor, smoke, or visible damage is present, unplug the device, remove it from service, and escalate immediately. Stop.

steps.title: Repeat Self-Test After Basic Corrections
steps.instructions: After confirming AC power, battery charge, helium supply, external cables, and accessories, restart the pump and allow the self-test to run again. Expected outcome: The pump either completes self-test normally or the failure remains reproducible. If the pump completes self-test, perform facility-required operational and safety checks before returning to service. Stop.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Datascope System 98 / 98XT intra-aortic balloon pump would power on but would not complete startup self-test.
CCR.Cause: Verified failure during self-test; AC outlet, power cord, battery charge, helium cylinder, and external cables were checked with no external cause found.
CCR.Resolution: Removed pump from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to persistent self-test failure.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested or alternate outlet used
Battery charge status or low-battery indication
Any displayed error message or alarm
Point in self-test where failure occurred
Unusual heat, odor, smoke, or visible damage

title: Datascope System 98 / 98XT Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Battery Or AC Power Failure
description: Troubleshooting AC power loss, battery charging problems, short battery runtime, or failure to power on safely.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Datascope System 98 / 98XT is connected to a patient or being prepared for active IABP therapy. If the device is in use and AC or battery power is questionable, immediately notify clinical staff and ensure backup IABP support is available. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained without relying on a pump with unresolved power reliability concerns. If safe backup support is not immediately available, do not continue nonessential troubleshooting at the bedside.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Power Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact complaint: Will not power on; Powers on with AC only; Powers on with battery only; Battery does not charge; Battery runtime is short; Power drops out when moved; AC power indicator does not illuminate; Low battery or battery failure alarm occurs. Expected outcome: The issue is clearly defined before parts or internal failure are suspected.

steps.title: Check the Wall Outlet
steps.instructions: Plug another approved test device or outlet tester into the same receptacle. Confirm the outlet is live and not controlled by a wall switch, emergency circuit issue, loose receptacle, or tripped power strip. Expected outcome: Facility power is verified. If the outlet is dead, move the IABP to a verified outlet and notify Facilities as appropriate. If this resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Inspect the AC Power Cord
steps.instructions: Check the power cord for cuts, crushed insulation, bent blades, loose plug fit, strain relief damage, or signs of overheating. Confirm the cord is fully seated at the wall and at the IABP power inlet. Expected outcome: The cord is secure and physically safe to use. If the cord is damaged, remove it from service and replace it with the correct hospital-approved cord. If this resolves the issue, stop.

steps.title: Check the AC Power Indicator and Startup Behavior
steps.instructions: Connect the pump to a verified AC outlet and observe the AC power indicator, display response, fan noise, charger behavior, and startup sequence. Expected outcome: The unit recognizes AC power and starts normally. If there is no AC indicator on a known-good outlet with a known-good cord, suspect an internal power supply, inlet, fuse, or charging circuit issue.

steps.title: Confirm Power Switch Position and Basic Startup
steps.instructions: Verify the main power switch is in the correct position and that the pump is not being mistaken for a display or battery-only failure. Attempt a normal startup while connected to verified AC power. Expected outcome: The pump powers on without interruption. If the unit powers briefly and then shuts down, document the timing and any alarms or indicator behavior.

steps.title: Allow Charging Time if Battery Was Fully Depleted
steps.instructions: If the pump has been stored unplugged or unused, connect it to verified AC power and allow the battery to charge per facility practice. Observe whether the charging indicator responds. Expected outcome: The battery begins charging and the device remains stable on AC power. If the battery does not appear to charge after being connected to verified AC power, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Battery Runtime Safely
steps.instructions: Only perform battery runtime checks when the pump is not connected to a patient. With the unit charged and functioning on AC power, briefly disconnect AC power and confirm whether the pump transfers to battery operation without shutting down. Expected outcome: The pump remains powered and indicates battery operation. If the unit shuts down, alarms immediately, or cannot support battery operation, remove it from service.

steps.title: Inspect External Battery Indicators and Alarms
steps.instructions: Note any battery-related messages, low battery alarms, charger alarms, or abnormal indicator behavior. Do not silence or ignore power alarms during evaluation. Expected outcome: Alarm behavior is captured clearly for repair documentation. If repeated battery alarms occur after charging on verified AC power, suspect battery age, battery pack failure, charger failure, or internal power management issue.

steps.title: Check for Power Dropout During Movement
steps.instructions: While the pump is not connected to a patient, gently move the AC cord at the plug, device inlet, and strain relief area. Do not force connectors or open the unit. Expected outcome: The device remains powered without flicker, reboot, or alarm. If power drops out when the cord or inlet is moved, remove the pump from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Inspect for Heat, Odor, or Electrical Damage
steps.instructions: Check for unusual heat, burning smell, discoloration, buzzing, sparking, melted plastic, or damaged inlet areas. Expected outcome: No signs of electrical hazard are present. If any electrical hazard is found, unplug the device if safe, label it Out of Service, and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Power Cord and Outlet
steps.instructions: If available, repeat the AC power check using a known-good hospital-grade cord and verified outlet. Expected outcome: The issue follows either the cord/outlet or remains with the pump. If the pump works normally with the known-good cord and outlet, replace the defective external item and document the correction. If the issue remains, stop troubleshooting and escalate.

steps.title: Review Battery Age and Service History
steps.instructions: Check the asset record for prior battery replacements, charging complaints, power supply repairs, or repeated power-related work orders. Expected outcome: History supports whether this is a recurring battery or power system failure. If battery age is excessive or repeated failures are documented, include that information in the repair request.

steps.title: Do Not Perform Internal Power Supply Repair at the Bedside
steps.instructions: Do not open the pump, bypass fuses, defeat alarms, modify power wiring, or attempt internal charger repair during field troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting remains safe and limited to Clinical Engineering external checks. If external checks do not resolve the issue, remove the pump from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Datascope System 98 / 98XT would not maintain battery operation and displayed a low battery condition when unplugged from AC power.
CCR.Cause: Verified wall outlet and AC cord were functional, but the pump failed to sustain operation on battery after charging.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the IABP from service, labeled it Out of Service, and sent it for bench evaluation of the battery and charging system.

helpfulDetails: Low battery alarm or message observed
Whether unit powers on with AC
Whether unit powers on with battery
Whether power drops out when moved
Battery age or last replacement date
Any unusual heat, smell, sound, or visible damage
Whether backup IABP support was required

title: Datascope System 98 / 98XT Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - ECG/AP Trigger Instability
description: Troubleshooting unstable ECG or arterial pressure triggering caused by patient leads, cables, transducers, connections, signal quality, or trigger setup.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is hemodynamically supported and that clinical staff are aware the IABP trigger is unstable. If balloon timing, assist ratio, or augmentation is unreliable, have qualified clinical staff place the patient on the safest available support mode or prepare alternate support while troubleshooting. Expected: The patient remains supported and clinical staff are actively managing therapy. If the trigger issue creates unsafe balloon timing, stop troubleshooting on the active patient and escalate immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Trigger Problem
steps.instructions: Observe the Datascope System 98 / 98XT display and confirm whether the issue involves: ECG trigger loss; ECG trigger jumping between beats; AP trigger loss; AP waveform dampening; Irregular assist timing; Trigger alarms or intermittent trigger source changes; Trigger instability only during movement, transport, or lead handling. Expected: The exact trigger behavior is confirmed before parts are swapped.

steps.title: Check ECG Lead Placement and Skin Contact
steps.instructions: Inspect ECG electrodes for loose pads, dried gel, poor skin prep, hair interference, sweat, blood, lotion, or lifted edges. Replace questionable electrodes and confirm the leads are placed securely. Expected: ECG waveform becomes clean, stable, and consistently detected. If stable triggering returns, document the electrode issue and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the ECG Leadwire Set
steps.instructions: Check the ECG leadwires for bent pins, cracked insulation, stretched wires, contamination, or intermittent response when gently moved. Swap with a known-good compatible ECG leadwire set if available. Expected: ECG waveform remains stable when the cable is handled gently. If swapping the leadwire resolves the issue, replace the defective leadwire set and stop.

steps.title: Confirm ECG Signal Quality
steps.instructions: Look for low-amplitude QRS complexes, excessive artifact, baseline wander, pacing spikes, poor lead selection, or motion artifact. Work with clinical staff to select the cleanest available ECG lead for triggering. Expected: The IABP detects a consistent R-wave without double-counting or missed beats. If the ECG signal remains poor due to patient condition or rhythm, continue evaluating AP trigger as appropriate.

steps.title: Check ECG Input Connection at the Pump
steps.instructions: Verify the ECG cable is fully seated at the IABP input and that the connector is not loose, damaged, dirty, or strained. Gently secure the cable to reduce pull on the connector. Expected: ECG signal does not drop out when the connector area is lightly moved. If the ECG input is physically loose or intermittent at the pump, remove the device from service when clinically safe.

steps.title: Review Trigger Source Selection
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the pump is set to ECG trigger, AP trigger, or another available trigger mode. Verify that the selected trigger source matches the best available patient signal. Expected: The selected trigger source provides reliable timing and consistent assist. If the wrong trigger source was selected and changing it resolves the issue, document the setup correction and stop.

steps.title: Evaluate Arterial Pressure Waveform Quality
steps.instructions: If AP trigger is being used, inspect the arterial pressure waveform for damping, whip, noise, low amplitude, leveling issues, or flush artifact. Confirm with clinical staff that the arterial line and transducer are properly set up and clinically valid. Expected: The AP waveform has a sharp, consistent upstroke suitable for trigger detection. If AP waveform correction resolves the trigger instability, document the external waveform cause and stop.

steps.title: Inspect the Pressure Transducer Cable and Connection
steps.instructions: Check the AP transducer cable from the patient setup to the pump for loose connections, damaged connectors, fluid contamination, pin damage, or intermittent signal during gentle movement. Swap the pressure cable or transducer interface cable with a known-good compatible cable if available. Expected: AP waveform remains stable and trigger timing becomes consistent. If cable replacement resolves the issue, remove the defective cable from service and stop.

steps.title: Check for External Interference or Cable Routing Problems
steps.instructions: Verify ECG and pressure cables are not tightly wrapped around power cords, bed motors, warming devices, infusion pumps, or other equipment that may introduce noise. Re-route cables to reduce strain, crossing, and electrical noise. Expected: Artifact decreases and trigger detection improves. If cable routing resolves the issue, document the correction and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Pump Power and Grounding Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet when AC power is being used. Check for damaged power cord, loose plug fit, unusual power behavior, or instability when switching between AC and battery operation. Expected: The pump powers normally without signal noise changing due to power source issues. If power-related instability is suspected, remove the pump from service after patient care is safely transferred.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good IABP or Signal Source if Available
steps.instructions: When clinically appropriate, compare the same ECG/AP patient signal with another verified IABP or bedside monitor. This helps determine whether the instability follows the patient signal/cable setup or remains with the Datascope System 98 / 98XT pump. Expected: The fault is isolated to either external signal setup or the pump. If the issue remains only with the System 98 / 98XT, remove it from service for bench evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Safe Visual Inspection of the Pump
steps.instructions: Inspect the pump exterior, ECG/AP connectors, display behavior, alarm messages, and cable strain points. Look for signs of impact, fluid intrusion, damaged connectors, missing strain relief, unusual heat, odor, or inconsistent front-panel response. Expected: No obvious external damage or unsafe condition is present. If damage, fluid intrusion, or abnormal operation is found, label the pump Out of Service.

steps.title: Do Not Continue Therapy on an Unstable Trigger Source
steps.instructions: If ECG and AP trigger instability persists after external leads, cables, signal quality, setup, power, and connections are checked, do not assume the device is safe for continued clinical use. Expected: The device is removed from active use once common external causes have been ruled out.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Datascope System 98 / 98XT IABP was intermittently losing ECG/AP trigger and assisting inconsistently during patient support.
CCR.Cause: ECG electrodes and leadwire were checked, AP transducer cable was reseated and swapped, and trigger instability remained isolated to the IABP input.
CCR.Resolution: Pump was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to persistent trigger instability after external causes were ruled out.

helpfulDetails: Outlet tested or alternate outlet used
Selected trigger source: ECG or AP
Any unusual heat, odor, noise, or fluid exposure
Whether issue followed cable/accessory or stayed with pump

title: Datascope System 98 / 98XT Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Helium Cylinder or Pneumatic Leak Fault
description: Troubleshooting helium supply, cylinder seating, tubing, connector, or external pneumatic leak issues on Datascope System 98 / 98XT IABPs.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Datascope System 98 / 98XT is connected to an active patient. If the IABP is supporting a patient and there is a helium or pneumatic leak fault, do not continue extended troubleshooting at bedside. Notify clinical staff immediately and have a backup IABP available or transfer support per facility policy. Expected: Patient support is maintained without relying on a device with an unresolved pneumatic fault.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Verify the exact alarm, fault message, or behavior reported by clinical staff. Look for symptoms such as: Helium cylinder empty or low indication; Unable to build pneumatic pressure; Repeated helium loss alarm; Balloon not inflating properly; Pump cycling but no effective assist; Audible hiss from cylinder, regulator area, hose, or rear panel. Expected: The helium or pneumatic fault is confirmed before parts are swapped.

steps.title: Check Helium Cylinder Level
steps.instructions: Inspect the installed helium cylinder and verify it is not empty or near empty. Replace the cylinder with a known full, approved helium cylinder if the pressure appears low or uncertain. Expected: If the issue was caused by a depleted cylinder, the fault clears after cylinder replacement and normal pneumatic operation returns. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Cylinder Is Fully Seated
steps.instructions: Remove and reinstall the helium cylinder following normal external handling procedures. Confirm the cylinder is seated squarely, tightened correctly, and not cross-threaded or partially engaged. Expected: A properly seated cylinder should seal without leakage and allow the IABP to pressurize normally. If resolved, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Cylinder Seal and Connection Area
steps.instructions: Check the cylinder connection area for damaged threads, missing or damaged sealing surfaces, debris, or signs of gas leakage. Do not use damaged cylinders or damaged connection hardware. Expected: The cylinder connection should be clean, intact, and free of visible damage. Damaged cylinders should be removed from use.

steps.title: Listen for External Leakage
steps.instructions: With the device powered and attempting normal pneumatic operation, listen near the helium cylinder connection, rear pneumatic fittings, catheter connection area, and accessible external tubing. Do not open the unit or perform internal pneumatic disassembly. Expected: No continuous hissing should be heard. A clear external leak location may identify a cylinder, fitting, tubing, or connection problem.

steps.title: Check the IAB Catheter Pneumatic Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the balloon catheter tubing connection to the pump. Confirm the catheter extension tubing is fully connected, not kinked, cracked, loose, or contaminated. Expected: The catheter pneumatic path should be secure and unobstructed. If reseating the connection restores operation, document the finding and return the device only after appropriate functional verification.

steps.title: Check for Kinks or Damage in External Tubing
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible external pneumatic tubing for sharp bends, pinching, stretching, cracks, or loose fittings. Pay close attention to tubing routed around bed rails, transport hardware, or the IABP cart. Expected: Tubing should be intact and routed without restriction. If corrected and the fault clears, stop troubleshooting after confirming normal operation.

steps.title: Swap Known-Good External Accessories When Safe
steps.instructions: If available and appropriate, use a known-good helium cylinder and approved external tubing or connection accessories. Do not swap patient-connected components unless clinical staff confirms it is safe and clinically appropriate. Expected: If the fault follows an accessory, remove that accessory from service. If the fault stays with the IABP, suspect an internal pneumatic issue.

steps.title: Verify Device Startup and Self-Test Behavior
steps.instructions: Power cycle the device only when it is not supporting a patient or when clinical staff has confirmed it is safe. Observe whether the helium or pneumatic fault appears immediately at startup or only during pumping. Expected: A fault present immediately at startup with a known full cylinder and secure connections suggests an internal pneumatic or sensing problem.

steps.title: Check for Signs of Regulator or Internal Pneumatic Failure
steps.instructions: If the cylinder is full, properly seated, and no external leak is found, note whether the pump still fails to pressurize, vents helium rapidly, or repeatedly alarms during assist. Expected: Persistent failure after external checks points toward an internal pneumatic leak, regulator issue, valve problem, pressure sensor issue, or internal tubing failure.

steps.title: Remove From Service if the Fault Persists
steps.instructions: If the fault remains after checking cylinder level, cylinder seating, visible pneumatic connections, catheter tubing, and external accessories, remove the IABP from service. Expected: The device is not returned to clinical use until evaluated and repaired by qualified service personnel.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported the Datascope System 98 / 98XT displayed a helium cylinder or pneumatic leak fault and would not maintain balloon assist pressure.
CCR.Cause: A full helium cylinder was installed and external cylinder seating, catheter tubing, and visible pneumatic connections were checked, but the fault remained with the pump.
CCR.Resolution: IABP was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to suspected internal pneumatic leak or regulator failure.

helpfulDetails: Helium cylinder pressure or full-cylinder replacement confirmed
Cylinder connection inspected for damaged threads or seal issues
Audible hiss present or not present
Fault occurred at startup or only during pumping
Catheter tubing checked for kinks, cracks, or loose connection
External tubing or accessory swapped, if applicable
Any unusual venting, cycling, or pressure behavior
Final device status: returned to use, removed from service, or sent for repair

title: Datascope System 98 / 98XT Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump - Recorder / Display / Control Panel Failure
description: Troubleshooting recorder, display, or control panel problems that affect visibility, documentation, or operator input on the IABP.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the Datascope System 98 / 98XT is connected to an active patient. If the IABP is supporting a patient and the display or controls are unreliable, do not continue troubleshooting at bedside unless clinical leadership confirms it is safe. Have perfusion, anesthesia, ICU, or cath lab staff prepare another verified IABP if therapy visibility or control is compromised. Expected outcome: Patient support is maintained without relying on a pump with questionable display or control function.

steps.title: Identify the Exact Failure
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue involves: Recorder not printing; Recorder paper feed failure; Blank or dim display; Intermittent display; Frozen screen; Control panel buttons not responding; Knob or keypad input failure; Incorrect or delayed screen response. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to recorder, display, control input, or a combined user-interface issue.

steps.title: Check AC Power and Battery Status
steps.instructions: Verify the pump is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Confirm the power cord is fully seated and not damaged. Observe whether the device is running on AC power or battery. If the display dims, flickers, or resets when power is moved or when switching between AC and battery, document that behavior. Expected outcome: Power instability is ruled out as the cause of display or panel failure.

steps.title: Inspect the Display Area Externally
steps.instructions: Check the screen for cracks, fluid intrusion, impact damage, loose bezel areas, heavy residue, or signs of cleaning chemical damage. Do not remove covers or disassemble the front panel during basic troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Obvious physical or liquid damage is identified before further testing.

steps.title: Check Brightness, Contrast, and Viewing Conditions
steps.instructions: If the screen appears dim or hard to read, confirm the issue is not due to ambient lighting, glare, or an adjusted display setting. Adjust only normal user-accessible brightness or contrast controls if available. Expected outcome: The display becomes readable if the issue was setting-related. If this resolves the issue, return the device to service only after confirming normal operation.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Power Cycle if Safe
steps.instructions: If the pump is not connected to an active patient, power the unit down using the normal power-off process. Wait briefly, then restart the pump and observe the startup sequence. Confirm whether the display initializes normally and whether controls respond during startup. Expected outcome: Temporary software or startup lockup clears, or the failure repeats and is documented.

steps.title: Verify Control Panel Response
steps.instructions: With the device off patient use, press several normal front-panel buttons and observe whether each input is recognized. Check for buttons that are stuck, physically damaged, unusually loose, or require excessive force. Do not continue pressing a stuck control repeatedly, especially if it could affect therapy settings during use. Expected outcome: The control panel either responds normally or a specific input failure is confirmed.

steps.title: Check for Contamination Around Controls
steps.instructions: Inspect the control panel for adhesive residue, dried fluid, cracked overlay, cleaning residue, or debris around buttons and knobs. Clean only according to approved facility cleaning practices and allow the surface to dry fully before retesting. Expected outcome: External contamination is ruled out as the cause of poor button response.

steps.title: Evaluate Recorder Paper and Door
steps.instructions: If the recorder does not print or feed paper, check that compatible recorder paper is installed correctly. Confirm the paper door is fully closed and latched. Look for jammed paper, torn paper, or paper loaded in the wrong direction. Expected outcome: The recorder feeds and prints normally if the issue was paper loading or door position. If this resolves the issue, document the correction and return the unit to service after verifying print quality.

steps.title: Inspect the Recorder Output
steps.instructions: Print a test strip or available waveform record only when the pump is not actively supporting a patient or when clinical workflow allows. Check whether the recorder output is blank, faint, distorted, incomplete, or not advancing. Expected outcome: Recorder failure is narrowed to no print, poor print quality, or paper transport issue.

steps.title: Check for External Accessories or Cables Affecting Operation
steps.instructions: Inspect external cables connected to the pump, including ECG, pressure, power, and any output/interface connections. A damaged or partially connected external cable may create confusing behavior or interrupt normal monitoring display. Disconnect nonessential external accessories only if the pump is not in active patient use and it is safe to do so. Expected outcome: External connection issues are ruled out before suspecting internal display or control failure.

steps.title: Look for Startup Errors, Alarms, or Self-Test Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe whether the pump displays any startup error, failed self-test, warning message, abnormal beep pattern, or incomplete boot sequence. Document the exact message or behavior. Expected outcome: Any visible failure pattern is captured for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Compare With a Known-Good Unit if Available
steps.instructions: If another Datascope System 98 / 98XT is available, compare normal startup, display brightness, button response, and recorder function. Do not swap internal parts. Expected outcome: The suspect unit’s behavior is confirmed as abnormal compared with a working pump.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Device Is Safe to Return to Service
steps.instructions: The device should not be returned to service if: Display is blank, unreadable, or intermittent; Controls are delayed, stuck, or nonresponsive; Therapy settings cannot be clearly viewed or adjusted; Recorder failure affects required documentation during patient care; Any error repeats after basic checks; Physical damage, fluid intrusion, heat, odor, or abnormal startup behavior is present. Expected outcome: Device status is based on patient safety and reliable operation, not partial functionality.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Datascope System 98 / 98XT recorder would not print and the front display intermittently dimmed during setup.
CCR.Cause: Recorder paper was loaded correctly and AC power was verified, but the display remained intermittent and the control panel response was delayed after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Device was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for bench evaluation due to suspected internal display/control panel failure.

helpfulDetails: Whether the pump was in patient use
Outlet tested and AC indicator status
Display behavior: blank, dim, flickering, frozen, or intermittent
Control panel behavior: stuck, delayed, or nonresponsive buttons
Recorder behavior: no feed, no print, faint print, jam, or paper error
Recorder paper condition and loading direction
Any startup errors or abnormal tones
Signs of impact, fluid intrusion, unusual heat, smell, or residue
Whether the issue changed after restart
